title: Zeus Takes It Seriously
tagline: Zeus has entered everything and won nothing. That was fine. Then something changed. He wants to be genuinely good at one thing.
arc_note: ZEUS CHARACTER GROWTH — He has always been the enthusiastic one who enters everything and finishes last with a huge smile. This episode is the first time we see him want something beyond participation. His father's letter (EP015) shifted something. He's going to be needed. He wants to be ready. He picks: the thunderbolt.

scenes.title: The Decision
scenes.narration: Zeus made the decision without telling anyone. He went to the practice field at dawn, before anyone was awake, and he practiced his thunderbolt aim. He wasn't bad — he was fine. He could hit general areas reliably. What he couldn't do: precision. Specific, controlled, exactly-where-intended. He'd never needed it before. He needed it now. He didn't know why yet. He just knew.
scenes.visual_prompt: Little Olympus kids cartoon style. Zeus alone at dawn on the practice field — no audience, no fanfare. He fires thunderbolts at specific targets: hits general area, misses precise center. He resets. Fires again. Resets. Nobody watching. This is new behavior. Pre-dawn light, determination on his face. 16:9.

scenes.title: He Asks Achilles
scenes.narration: He didn't ask Athena — she would make a twelve-page plan. He didn't ask Hercules — the approach would be enthusiastic. He asked Achilles, because Achilles had learned to coach in EP005 and had spent EP013 thinking about precision. Zeus found him before the morning run. He said: 'I need to learn to aim.' Achilles looked at him. He said: 'Aim at what?' Zeus said: 'Anything. With control.' Achilles said: 'Okay. Come back at noon. I'll watch you.'
scenes.visual_prompt: Little Olympus kids cartoon style. Zeus approaching Achilles before the morning run — a private conversation. Zeus asking directly, simply. Achilles's surprise at being asked, then his immediate pivot to coach mode: 'I'll watch you.' A new dynamic — Zeus as student, Achilles as coach. 16:9.

scenes.title: Achilles Watches
scenes.narration: Achilles watched Zeus throw thunderbolts for forty minutes without saying anything. He watched the footwork, the release point, the follow-through. He made notes on a small tablet — numbers, angles, timing. Then he said five things. Specific things: different stance, different release point, shorter wind-up, look through the target not at it, breathe out on release. Zeus tried each one. By the fifth attempt with the new form: he hit the center of the target. He stared at it. He said: 'Oh.'
scenes.visual_prompt: Little Olympus kids cartoon style. Achilles watching Zeus throw, taking notes, saying nothing for a long time. Then five specific instructions. Zeus adjusting step by step. Fifth attempt: center hit. Zeus staring at the target. 'Oh.' — the sound of something clicking into place. Achilles allowing himself a small nod. 16:9.

scenes.title: Two Weeks
scenes.narration: He practiced every day for two weeks. Dawn sessions, noon sessions with Achilles, sometimes evening sessions alone. The others noticed but nobody said anything until Perseus said, at dinner: 'Zeus has been hitting every target today.' Hercules said: 'I know, I watched! He hit the small one!' Athena said nothing. She had noticed on day three. She had let him have it as his own thing. Archie looked at Zeus. Zeus didn't notice. He was thinking about his release point.
scenes.visual_prompt: Little Olympus kids cartoon style. TWO-WEEK MONTAGE — Zeus at dawn, at noon with Achilles, in the evening alone. Each session: better. At dinner: Perseus and Hercules noticing and celebrating. Athena not saying she noticed on day three. Zeus thinking about technique at the dinner table. 16:9.

scenes.title: Why He Needed This
scenes.narration: At the end of the second week, Achilles asked: 'Why thunderbolts? You could have practiced anything.' Zeus looked at the practice field. He said: 'My father said we'll know when it's time. For the underground thing.' He didn't elaborate. Achilles said: 'And when it's time?' Zeus said: 'I want to be useful. Not entered. Useful.' He threw one more bolt. Center. Clean. He picked up his crown and left. Achilles watched him go.
scenes.visual_prompt: Little Olympus kids cartoon style. Zeus and Achilles at the practice field at dusk. The honest exchange. Zeus's reason: he wants to be useful, not just enthusiastic. One final perfect throw. He picks up his crown and leaves. Achilles watching him go with an expression of quiet respect. 16:9.

scenes.title: What He Can Do Now
scenes.narration: By week three, Zeus could hit a specific target at forty meters with consistent precision. Not perfect — he still missed sometimes. But controlled. Intentional. He entered the next inter-Olympus competition — not to win, but to test his aim under pressure. He came sixth. He hit every target he aimed at. He didn't beam quite the same way he used to. Something more specific and quieter had replaced it.
scenes.visual_prompt: Little Olympus kids cartoon style. Competition — Zeus at the thunderbolt accuracy event. Sixth place. But every target he aimed at: hit. His expression isn't the usual enormous grin — it's a smaller, more specific satisfaction. He knows what he can do now. That's different from enjoying participation. 16:9.

scenes.title: What Did We Learn?
scenes.narration: There's a difference between participating for joy and practicing for purpose. Zeus had both, always, but this was the first time he reached for the second. Wanting to be ready for something — even something you can't name yet — is a kind of preparation that matters. See you next time on Little Olympus!
scenes.visual_prompt: Little Olympus kids cartoon style. Final card — Zeus at the practice field at dawn again, alone, throwing. Precise. Settled. The bent crown on his head, straight now — he's wearing it differently. The mountain behind him, stable. Something is coming. He is getting ready. 16:9.

lesson: There's joy in participation and purpose in preparation. Learning to want both — and knowing which one you need right now — is growing up.
zeus_arc_note: EP017: Zeus becomes genuinely competent with precision thunderbolts. Critical for EP020 finale. The shift from 'entering everything' to 'getting good at one thing' is his season arc completion.
youtube_title: Zeus Decides He Wants to Be ACTUALLY Good at Something ⚡ | Little Olympus Episode 17
next_episode_preview: Episode 18 — Athena's Warning! She's finished her scroll. She knows what's coming. She needs to tell everyone.
